ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'bubble tea' class (FOODON_03311737); no specific 'less sugar' or 'flavoured milk' variant exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers tea-based beverages with milk; no dedicated bubble tea or boba sub-code exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for bubble tea or flavoured milk tea found in published SNOMED CT release as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one medium takeaway cup of approximately 500 ml, equivalent to 500 g, as commonly sold at bubble tea shops in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Flavoured bubble tea with milk (less sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Flavoured bubble tea with milk (less sugar)', CR ID D08010028. All per-100g values are as reported. Saturated fat not separately reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from analogous milk tea composition using USDA FoodData Central reference (see notes).

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sweetened Beverages and Milk Tea — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened milk tea and sugar-sweetened beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for 'flavoured bubble tea with milk (less sugar)' in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 is estimated from the closest analogous entries: sweetened milk tea (GI ~65–75) and sugar-sweetened beverages with similar sugar content (~70). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 73.65) / 100 = 51.6, based on a 500 g serving containing 73.65 g carbohydrate. Input record GI confidence was 0.349, reflecting low direct evidence; this analogous estimate is consistent with that assessment.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (11.83 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage due to its 11.83 g/100 g sugar content, which exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold associated with elevated blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines; request 0% sugar or choose unsweetened tea instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI sugar-sweetened beverage with 11.83 g/100 g sugar and 14.73 g/100 g carbohydrate triggers rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage as it qualifies as a high-GI sugar-sweetened beverage (GI ~70, sugar 11.83 g/100 g); the ADA SSB hard gate applies to 'less sugar' variants whenever sugar exceeds zero, and a 500 ml serving delivers approximately 73.65 g carbohydrate with negligible fibre buffering.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combination of high sugar load, likely saturated and trans fat from non-dairy creamer, and absence of cardioprotective nutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this drink due to its high sugar content (11.83 g/100 g), estimated saturated fat (~1.5 g/100 g) and potential trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) from non-dairy creamer, all of which contribute to dyslipidaemia and endothelial dysfunction; choose unsweetened, dairy-free tea alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory profile: high sugar, likely trans fat from non-dairy creamer, and absence of anti-inflammatory nutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage as its high sugar content (11.83 g/100 g) and potential trans fat from non-dairy creamer promote inflammatory cytokine activity; choose unsweetened green tea or anti-inflammatory beverages instead.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured Bubble Tea with Milk (Less Sugar) is a freshly prepared Taiwanese-origin beverage widely sold across Singapore and Southeast Asia. It consists of a flavoured tea base (commonly taro, matcha, strawberry, or other fruit/floral flavours) blended with non-dairy creamer or milk, sweetened with a reduced amount of sugar syrup, and optionally topped with chewy tapioca pearls (boba). The 'less sugar' designation typically corresponds to approximately 30–50% of the standard sugar level used by the outlet, but sugar content remains substantial at 11.83 g per 100 g. The beverage has a creamy, mildly sweet flavour with a smooth texture; tapioca pearls add a chewy, gelatinous mouthfeel. Per 100 g, it provides 80 kcal, 0.34 g protein, 2.41 g fat, 14.73 g carbohydrate, 11.83 g sugar, 0.03 g dietary fibre, and 13 mg sodium. Despite the reduced-sugar label, this remains a high-GI (GI ~70) sugar-sweetened beverage with negligible fibre, making it clinically unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Non-dairy creamer used in most commercial preparations may contain palm oil-derived saturated fat and partially hydrogenated oil-derived trans fat, adding further cardiovascular risk. A standard 500 ml serving delivers approximately 400 kcal and 73.65 g carbohydrate. Consumers seeking a healthier option should request 0% sugar and avoid tapioca pearl toppings to minimise glycaemic and caloric impact.
common_user_queries: How many calories in bubble tea?
Is less sugar bubble tea safe for hypertension?
What is the GI of bubble tea?
Is boba tea suitable for weight loss?
How much sugar is in less sugar bubble tea?
Can I drink bubble tea if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID D08010028) with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (confidence medium) as no direct bubble tea entry exists. Saturated fat estimated from analogous USDA milk tea composition. Overall record confidence is high for macros and secondary nutrients; medium for GI and fatty acid breakdown.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.50 g/100 g) is estimated from analogous non-dairy creamer-based milk tea composition using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Beverages, tea, ready-to-drink, milk tea' as a proxy; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. 2. Trans fat (0.10 g/100 g) is estimated based on typical non-dairy creamer formulations containing partially hydrogenated vegetable oil; this value is approximate and may vary by outlet. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this freshly prepared commercial bubble tea variant are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 4. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for flavoured bubble tea with milk (less sugar) exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 5. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (70 × 73.65 g carbohydrate per 500 g serving) / 100 = 51.6. 6. The 'less sugar' designation does not eliminate clinical risk; sugar content of 11.83 g/100 g still qualifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage under ADA, AHA, and WHO definitions.
